C:
Short answer: yes, but you almost certainly want static generation (SSG/prerendering), not per-request SSR. For a content-heavy marketing site, server-rendered HTML is worth it — the complexity that isn't worth it is running a live render server when a build step would do the same job.

Why server-rendering wins for this specific case. Marketing sites live or die on three things that client-side rendering (a blank `<div>` hydrated by JavaScript) handles poorly:

- **SEO and crawlability.** Google renders JS now, but inconsistently and on a delay; other crawlers and AI indexers often don't. Shipping real HTML removes that risk entirely. For a site whose whole purpose is being found, this is the decisive factor.
- **Social/link previews.** Open Graph and Twitter card tags need to be in the initial HTML response. Slack, iMessage, LinkedIn, and Facebook crawlers do not run your JavaScript. Client-rendered meta tags simply don't show up in shared links.
- **First paint and Core Web Vitals.** Content appears immediately instead of after a JS download-parse-execute cycle. On marketing pages this directly affects bounce rate and ad quality scores.

Why you probably don't need true SSR. "SSR" usually means rendering on the server *on every request*. That buys you per-request freshness and personalization — which a marketing site rarely needs. Your content changes when your team publishes, not when a visitor loads the page. So the better fit is **static generation**: render to HTML at build (or publish) time and serve it from a CDN. You get all three benefits above, plus better performance and far lower operational burden than a live render server — no Node process to keep alive, scale, or patch.

The complexity that actually scares people — hydration mismatches, server/client environment drift, a runtime you have to operate — mostly comes from *runtime* SSR. SSG sidesteps the operational half of it. A framework like Next.js, Astro, or SvelteKit gives you this with a config flag, and Astro in particular is built for exactly the content-heavy, low-interactivity case and ships almost no JS by default.

When you'd reach past SSG to real SSR:
- Content that must be fresh per request (live pricing, inventory, A/B tests that can't be done at the edge).
- Per-user personalization in the initial HTML (logged-in state, geo-targeted offers).
- A catalog so large that full rebuilds are impractical — though incremental/on-demand regeneration covers most of this without going fully dynamic.

Decision rule: default to static generation. Only adopt per-request SSR for the specific pages that need live or personalized data, and keep the rest static. That gives you the SEO and performance payoff while keeping the genuinely expensive complexity off the table.

- Content that must be fresh per request (live pricing, inventory, A/B tests that can't be done at the edge).
- Per-user personalization in the initial HTML (logged-in state, geo-targeted offers).
- A catalog so large that full rebuilds are impractical — though incremental/on-demand regeneration covers most of this without going fully dynamic.

Decision rule: default to static generation. Only adopt per-request SSR for the specific pages that need live or personalized data, and keep the rest static. That gives you the SEO and performance payoff while keeping the genuinely expensive complexity off the table.

What would change this answer. The whole recommendation rests on one assumption: that your content is mostly static and rebuildable, and your traffic mostly cares about SEO and first paint. Flip any of those and the calculus shifts. If most of your "marketing" pages are actually logged-in or per-visitor personalized at first paint, or your content updates faster than you can rebuild and your catalog is too large for incremental regeneration, then per-request SSR stops being the expensive exception and becomes the baseline. And the prior step matters too: if you discover crawlers and link-preview bots already handle your client-rendered pages acceptably (worth verifying with Google's URL Inspection tool and a real Slack/LinkedIn share test), then even SSG's main payoff shrinks and a well-tuned SPA may be enough. The honest limitation: the SEO argument is strong but probabilistic — measure your actual crawl and indexing behavior before treating it as settled.
P:
Short answer: yes — but what's worth it is server-*rendered HTML*, and you almost certainly want it produced at build time (static generation / prerendering), not by a live render server on every request. The HTML payoff is large for a marketing site; the complexity that genuinely bites comes from *runtime* SSR, and you can take the payoff while leaving that on the table.

**Why server-rendered HTML wins here.** A content-heavy marketing site lives or dies on three things that client-side rendering — shipping a blank `<div>` and hydrating it with JavaScript — handles poorly:

- **SEO and crawlability.** Google does render JS, but inconsistently and on a delay; many other crawlers and AI indexers don't render it at all. Real HTML in the initial response removes that risk. For a site whose whole job is being found, this is the decisive factor.
- **Social and link previews.** Open Graph and Twitter Card tags have to be in the HTML the server returns. The crawlers behind Slack, iMessage, LinkedIn, and Facebook don't execute your JavaScript, so client-rendered meta tags produce blank or broken share cards.
- **First paint and Core Web Vitals.** Content shows up immediately instead of after a download-parse-execute cycle. On marketing pages that feeds bounce rate, conversion, and — if you run paid traffic — landing-page quality scores.

**Why static generation, not per-request SSR.** "SSR" usually means rendering on the server *on every request*. That buys per-request freshness and personalization, which a marketing site rarely needs: your pages change when your team publishes, not when a visitor loads them. So render to HTML at build (or publish) time and serve it from a CDN. You get all three benefits above, plus better performance and far lower operational burden — no Node process to keep alive, scale, or patch. The scary parts of SSR — hydration mismatches, server/client environment drift, a runtime you have to operate — are mostly *runtime* problems, and static generation sidesteps them. Next.js, SvelteKit, and Astro all do this behind a config flag; Astro is built for exactly the content-heavy, low-interactivity case and ships almost no JS by default.

**What static generation does cost** (so "worth the complexity" is an honest ledger, not a sales pitch): a build pipeline and framework conventions to learn; build times that grow with page count (incremental/on-demand builds mitigate this); and the fact that publishing now means a rebuild — so you wire your CMS's publish event to a rebuild webhook, or use on-demand regeneration. Real, but modest, and much smaller than operating a render server.

**When you'd reach past static generation to real SSR** — and ideally only for the specific pages that need it:
- Content that must be fresh per request (live pricing or inventory, A/B tests you can't run at the edge).
- Per-user personalization baked into the initial HTML (logged-in state, geo-targeted offers).
- A catalog so large that full rebuilds are impractical — though incremental/on-demand regeneration covers most of this without going fully dynamic.

**Decision rule:** default everything to static generation. Adopt per-request SSR only for the handful of pages that truly need live or personalized data, and keep the rest static. That captures the SEO and performance payoff while keeping the genuinely expensive complexity off the table.
